subject: [PATCH v4 0/4] arm64: cross-CPU NMI via SDEI

reviews.author: YinFengwei
reviews.summary:
Reviewer YinFengwei noted that the patch does not handle the case where SDEI is disabled in firmware, which would prevent the cross-CPU NMI mechanism from working.

Reviewer YinFengwei tested the patch on a Neoverse N2 based arm64 server and found that enabling pseudo-NMI (PMR) resulted in a significant increase in latency compared to disabling it, with an average delta of +73.5 ns/call.

Reviewer YinFengwei expressed strong agreement and support for the patchset, stating that it is necessary for existing systems and should be merged.
reviews.raw_body:
Hi Kirill
On Mon, Jun 22, 2026 at 02:56:16PM +0100, Kiryl Shutsemau wrote:

---

I tested the u-bench.c on a Neoverse N2 based arm64 server. The result
is as following:
   pseudo_nmi=0 (DAIF):       96.3  ns/call
   pseudo_nmi=1 (PMR):        169.8 ns/call
   delta:                     +73.5 ns/call

---

I couldn't agree more: We need a solution for existing system. And
like to see this patchset merged. Thanks.

Regards
Yin, Fengwei

reviews.author: Breno Leitao
reviews.summary: Reviewer Breno Leitao agrees with the patch and suggests maintaining it downstream if it doesn't get upstreamed, citing the trade-offs of pseudo-NMI performance overhead, losing debuggability by disabling NMI entirely, or carrying downstream patches as the least disruptive option for their business needs.
reviews.raw_body:
I completely agree. Should these patches unfortunately not make it upstream,
my plan is to maintain them downstream at Meta kernels until the Meta fleet
no longer contains any non-FEAT_NMI hosts.

The trade-offs under consideration are:

	1) Accept the performance overhead from pseudo-NMI
	2) Lose debuggability by disabling NMI entirely
	3) Maintain downstream patches

Of these three options, carrying downstream patches appears to be the least
disruptive path for our business needs.

reviews.author: Catalin Marinas
reviews.summary: Reviewer Catalin Marinas questioned whether firmware can handle a PSCI CPU_OFF call from the SDEI handler, which is one of the required calls for this mechanism to work.
reviews.raw_body:
Just to understand, your firmware cannot cope with a PSCI CPU_OFF from
the SDEI handler? This is one of the required calls to be supported.

-- 
Catalin

reviews.author: Catalin Marinas
reviews.summary: The reviewer noted that a dsb(ishst) instruction should be used instead of smp_wmb() to ensure proper ordering, citing the GIC IPI code as an example and expressing uncertainty about whether this is mandated by the SDEI spec.
reviews.raw_body:
The smp_wmb() is not sufficient here. In the GIC IPI code we use a
dsb(ishst). It should be similar here. I think TF-A does this already
but it's unclear from the SDEI spec that it is mandated.

-- 
Catalin

reviews.author: Catalin Marinas
reviews.summary: The reviewer, Catalin Marinas, questioned whether a barrier is needed in the patch, citing that backtrace_mask won't be visible without it, and referenced a similar concern raised in patch 4.
reviews.raw_body:
Does this need a barrier as in patch 4? Sashiko spotted that
backtrace_mask won't be visible without a dsb(ishst). Again, as per my
comment in patch 4, we get away with this because of the TF-A
implementation but that's not guaranteed (we could ask for the SDEI spec
to be updated assuming all firmware implementations do this).

-- 
Catalin

reviews.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
reviews.summary: The author investigated a hang issue on Grace that occurred when calling PSCI CPU_OFF after completing an SDEI event, and found that it persisted even with DAIF masked and the GIC PMR reset first. They acknowledged this as a separate issue from the main patch series and stated that it's only necessary for crash kernel reclamation, not required for the series.
reviews.raw_body:
I did chase it a fair bit. Bisecting on Grace: completing the event and
parking (no CPU_OFF) works, and so does the stack-switch + C-call setup
on its own. The hang only appears once I call PSCI CPU_OFF after the
event -- and it persists even with DAIF masked and the GIC PMR reset
first, so it isn't leftover interrupt/priority state from the dispatch.
It's a silent wedge: no TF-A exception report, nothing after the
last console line.

But I have not tried calling CPU_OFF directly, without completing the
event. I assumed it is required. Will give it a try when I have time.

Either way this is a side quest: it only lets a crash kernel reclaim the
stopped CPU. The dump itself is complete, so it's nice-to-have, not
required for the series.

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

reviews.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged a technical issue in patch 3/4, agreeing to add a barrier (dsb(ishst)) to the backtrace path to ensure correct ordering of operations.
reviews.raw_body:
You're right, I'll switch it to dsb(ishst) to match gic_ipi_send_mask().

And as you pointed, the xame fix is needed in 3/4: the backtrace path
publishes backtrace_mask before signalling and currently has no barrier
at all (as the bot spotted), so it needs the dsb(ishst) there too.

I'll also drop the now-incorrect "cumulative wmb" comment.

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

2026-06-26.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new mechanism to deliver NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt) across CPUs on arm64 architecture using SDEI (System Description Event Infrastructure). This is necessary for certain debug and observability features, such as capturing CPU backtraces and crash dumps, which require interrupting a CPU that has its interrupts locally masked. The existing pseudo-NMI mechanism is too expensive in terms of performance overhead, so this new approach uses SDEI to deliver the NMI at a lower cost. This allows for more accurate debugging and observability features without introducing significant performance regressions.
